docs.title: Overview — Piano Jazz Wiki
docs.text: Master synthesis of everything in this knowledge base. Updated as sources are ingested and the picture grows.

docs.title: Current state
docs.text:
Sources ingested: 1 of 9 raw sources.

What exists: - Core theory reference across all 12 keys — scales, diatonic chords, shell voicings, ii-V-I progressions - Foundational notions: intervals, modes, cadences, circle of fifths, shell voicings - Five pillar pages defining the learning curriculum (Scales, Chords, Voicings, Lead Sheets, Improvisation) - Beginner and intermediate practice plans with per-pillar exercises - Voicings system fully documented — all 5 types (Shell, Open, Rootless, Kenny Barron, Fourth) with concept pages - Melody-Matching technique documented - Julian Bradley artist page

What's missing: - 8 more raw sources not yet ingested (chord symbols guide, licks, blues materials) - Progressions and lick library - Concept pages for: ii-V-I deep dive, guide tones, bebop scales, reharmonization, blues form - Blues material integration (left-hand patterns, blues scale application)

docs.title: Big picture
docs.text:
Jazz piano mastery is built on five interlocking pillars. They are not independent — each reinforces the others:

  • Scales provide the tonal vocabulary for improvisation and help you navigate keys.
  • Chords define the harmonic language and are the foundation of comping and voice leading.
  • Voicings are how chords are realized on the piano — the same chord can sound thin or rich depending on voicing.
  • Lead Sheets translate the written jazz language (chord symbols + melody) into a performance.
  • Improvisation synthesizes all four above into spontaneous melodic expression.

The ii-V-I progression is the central building block of jazz harmony. Nearly everything else is a variation, extension, or reharmonization of it.

docs.title: Voicings picture (established)
docs.text: The five voicing types form a clear progression from beginner to advanced. Shell Voicings are the entry point; Rootless Voicings are the workhorse at intermediate level. Melody-Matching is the unifying technique for applying any voicing to a lead sheet. See Chord Voicing Principles for the three rules all voicings follow.

docs.text:

docs.title: Julian Bradley
docs.text: Jazz pianist and educator. Founder of Jazz Tutorial (jazztutorial.com). Known for clear, systematic teaching of jazz piano concepts with practical application focus.

docs.title: Teaching style
docs.text:
• Demonstrates everything from C, then shows how to adapt to all chord types and all 12 keys
  • Emphasizes interval patterns over note memorization
  • Advocates learning one concept at a time to depth before moving on
  • Strong focus on practical application: every concept is tied to when and how to use it

docs.title: Altered Dominants
docs.text: Dominant 7th chords (V7) can carry altered extensions — notes that are deliberately raised or lowered from the major scale. These create extra tension that wants to resolve into the I chord. All altered extensions live exclusively on V7 chords.

docs.title: The altered extensions
docs.text:
Extension Symbol Notes on C7 Character Flat 9 C7(b9) + Db Dark, menacing tension Sharp 9 C7(#9) + D#/Eb Bluesy, aggressive ("James Bond Chord") Sharp 11 C7(#11) + F# Lydian dominant flavor, bright tension Flat 13 C7(b13) + Ab Implies altered scale — see below

Multiple alterations can stack: C7(#11/b9), C7(b9/b13), etc.

docs.title: C7 altered
docs.text:
When you see C7(b13) or just "C7alt", it implies the full C altered scale — also called the Lydian Dominant b2 scale or the 7th mode of Db melodic minor:

C altered scale: C – Db – Eb – E – Gb – Ab – Bb

This scale contains all possible alterations simultaneously (b9, #9, #11, b13), so C7b13 is shorthand for "play everything altered."

docs.title: Where altered dominants appear
docs.text:
• On the V chord before resolution to a I chord (especially in minor ii-V-I)
  • As tritone substitutions — the altered dominant of a chord can be substituted with the dominant a tritone away
  • At phrase endings where extra harmonic tension is desired

docs.title: Minor ii-V-I example (in C minor):
docs.text:
ii:  Dø        (D half-diminished)
V:   G7(b9)    (altered dominant)
i:   Cm        (resolution)

docs.title: Practice approach
docs.text: Learn the voicings for altered V7 chords alongside standard V7 voicings. The Rootless Voicings system handles these well — for a C7 rootless voicing, you can substitute altered extensions for the 5th or 9th.

docs.text:

  • Rootless Voicings — how to voice altered dominants
  • Pillar - Chords — chord construction foundation

docs.title: Chord Symbols
docs.text: Complete reference for reading chord symbols in jazz lead sheets. Every symbol tells you the chord type and which extensions to add or avoid.

docs.title: The six 7th chord types
docs.text:
These are the core chord types. Learn their symbols, notes, and ii-V-I roles.

Symbol Name Notes (from C) Role in progressions Cmaj7, CΔ, C^ Major 7 C E G B I chord of major ii-V-I Cm7, C-7 Minor 7 C Eb G Bb ii chord of major ii-V-I C7 Dominant 7 C E G Bb V chord of any ii-V-I CmΔ, CmA Minor-major 7 C Eb G B I chord of minor ii-V-I Cø, Cm7b5 Half-diminished C Eb Gb Bb ii chord of minor ii-V-I C°7 Diminished 7 C Eb Gb A Passing/substitute chord

The three big ones (maj7, min7, V7) cover the vast majority of jazz standards. The others appear in minor ii-V-I progressions and as passing chords.

docs.title: 6th chords
docs.text:
The 6th replaces the 7th (contrast with 13th chords, which add the 6th alongside the 7th).

Symbol Name Notes (from C) Note C6 Major 6 C E G A Cm6 Minor 6 C Eb G A ⚠️ Uses major 6th (A), not Ab C6/9 Major 6/9 C E G A D Cm6/9 Minor 6/9 C Eb G A D

⚠️ Common trap: Cm6 has a natural 6th (A), not a flat 6th (Ab). The minor refers to the 3rd, not the 6th.

docs.title: 9th chords
docs.text:
Extensions are built from the major scale of the root — count up 9, 11, 13 from C.

Symbol Notes added Chord type Cmaj9 + major 9th (D) maj7 + 9 Cm9 + major 9th (D) min7 + 9 C9 + major 9th (D) V7 + 9 (C7 + D) CmΔ9 + major 9th (D) minor-major 7 + 9

docs.title: Altered 9ths (V7 chords only)
docs.text: Symbol Extension Sound C7(b9) Flat 9 (Db) Dark, tense C7(#9) Sharp 9 (D#/Eb) "James Bond Chord" — bluesy tension

docs.title: 11th chords
docs.text:
Notation rule: Use #4 for maj7 chords, use 11 for min7 and V7 chords.

Symbol Chord Extension Cmaj7#4, CΔ#4 Major 7 #4 = raised 11th (F#) Cm11 Minor 7 11 = perfect 11th (F) C7(#11) Dominant 7 #11 = raised 11th (F#) C7(#11/b9) Dominant 7 Both altered extensions

The 9th is often played along with the 11th even when not notated.

docs.title: Suspended 4th chords
docs.text:
The 4th replaces the 3rd (contrast with 11th chords, where 4th and 3rd coexist).

  • Applied almost exclusively to V7 chords: C7(sus4)
  • Common C7sus4 voicing: Bb F G C (from bottom)

docs.title: 13th chords
docs.text:
The 13th is the same pitch as the 6th — one octave higher. Only V7 chords use the symbol "13" (if the chord is major or minor, the same note is called the "6th").

Symbol Notes Note C13 C7 + 6th (A) "C13 is a C7 chord plus a 13th" C13(#11) C7 + #11 + 13 C7(b13) C7 + b13 (Ab) ⚠️ Implies C altered — see Altered Dominants

The 9th is often included below the 13th even when not notated.

docs.title: Slash chords
docs.text:
Format: Chord/Bass — the chord before the slash goes in the right hand; the bass note after the slash goes in the left hand.

Symbol Right hand Left hand Bb/C Bb chord C C/Db C chord Db Bbmaj7/C Bbmaj7 chord C

Slash chords are common for smooth bass lines and pedal-tone effects.

docs.title: Quick disambiguation table
docs.text: These look similar… But they're different because… 6th chord vs 13th chord 6th replaces 7th; 13th adds 6th to 7th Sus4 chord vs 11th chord Sus4 replaces 3rd; 11th adds 4th to 3rd #4 notation vs #11 Same note — #4 is for maj7 chords, #11 for min7/V7 Cm6 major 6th vs minor 6th Cm6 always uses major 6th (A natural), never Ab

docs.title: Chord Voicing Principles
docs.text: Three principles that apply to all professional jazz piano chord voicings. These are strong guidelines, not rigid rules — exceptions exist.

docs.title: 1. Play each note once (no repeated notes)
docs.text: Each finger contributes a new tonal color. If your left hand already has the 3rd, don't play the 3rd again in your right hand — use that finger to add a new color (9th, 11th, 13th, etc.).

docs.title: 2. Root, 3rd, and 7th voiced at the bottom
docs.text: These three notes define the chord type (maj7, min7, or V7). They carry more weight in the bass register, so they belong at the bottom of the voicing. Once they're established, extensions (9ths, 11ths, 13ths) are added higher up where they add color without confusing the listener.

docs.title: 3. Leave space for the melody
docs.text: The melody needs to be heard clearly above the chord. Leave a gap of at least a minor 3rd between the melody note (the highest note you're playing) and the note directly below it (which is part of the chord voicing). Without this gap, the chord sounds muddy and the melody gets lost.

docs.title: The five voicing types
docs.text:
These principles apply to all five types of jazz chord voicing:

Voicing Notes Hands Best use Shell Voicings 1 3 7 One Bare/minimal sound Open Voicings 1 5 3 7 (spread) Two Clean, with bass Rootless Voicings 3 5 7 9 Two Colorful, most versatile Kenny Barron Voicing 1 5 9 / 3 7 11 Two When melody is 11th/#11th Fourth Voicings 7 3 6 / 9 5 1 Two With band; modern sound

docs.title: Practice approach
docs.text:
• Learn one voicing type at a time — spend a week on each before moving to the next
  • Use side-slipping: start from C, then transpose the voicing up/down by various intervals until you can play it from all 12 notes
  • Count interval patterns, not note names (e.g., "root, up a major 3rd, up a minor 3rd" — not "C E G")
  • See Melody-Matching for how to choose which voicing to use when playing a lead sheet

docs.title: Fourth Voicings
docs.text: Voicings built in 4ths rather than 3rds. Traditional chords stack 3rds (C E G B D). Fourth voicings rearrange the same notes into a series of 4ths (B E A D G C), giving a more modern, open, quartal sound.

docs.title: Structure
docs.text:
Start from the chord's 7th and build upward in 4ths using notes from the scale. Three notes in each hand:

  • Left hand: 7 – 3 – 6 (three 4ths up from the 7th)
  • Right hand: 9 – 5 – 1 (continuing three more 4ths up)
  • Full: (7 3 6 / 9 5 1)

docs.title: C minor 7 (flatten 3rd and 7th, all other notes stay):
docs.text:
• LH: Bb – Eb – A (7 – 3 – 6)
  • RH: D – G – C (9 – 5 – 1)

docs.title: Suggested use
docs.text:
With a band (or bass player): Play the full two-handed version. The root can be left to the bassist.

Solo piano (sawn-off version): Drop the top two notes from the right hand and restructure: - LH: 1 – 7 (root and 7th) - RH: 3 – 6 – 9 (three upper color notes) - Full: (1 7 / 3 6 9)

Example for C major 7 solo: LH = C B, RH = E A D. The 9th (D) is the melody note on top — ideal when melody is the 9th.

docs.title: Sound character
docs.text: Fourth voicings have a distinctly modern, open quality. They sound less "lush" than rootless voicings and less grounded than open voicings. Common in post-bop and contemporary jazz piano.

docs.title: In the voicing system
docs.text: The fifth and most advanced voicing type in the Chord Voicing Principles system. See Melody-Matching for when to use them. Compare with Rootless Voicings for a more traditional colorful sound.

docs.title: Kenny Barron Voicing
docs.text: A two-handed chord voicing built from stacks of 5ths. Named after jazz pianist Kenny Barron — hear it at the opening of his composition Spiral. One of the few jazz voicings that genuinely requires large hands (you need to stretch a 9th in each hand).

docs.title: Structure
docs.text:
Both hands build stacks of 5ths. The left hand starts from the root; the right hand starts from the chord's 3rd:

  • Left hand: root → up a 5th → up another 5th (1 – 5 – 9)
  • Right hand: 3rd → up a 5th → up another 5th (3 – 7 – 11)

docs.title: C minor 11 example:
docs.text:
• LH: C – G – D (1 – 5 – 9)
  • RH: Eb – Bb – F (3 – 7 – 11)
  • Full voicing: (1 5 9 / 3 7 11)

docs.title: C major 7 #11 example (shift right hand up a half step):
docs.text:
• LH: C – G – D (1 – 5 – 9)
  • RH: E – B – F# (3 – 7 – #11)
  • Full voicing: (1 5 9 / 3 7 #11)

docs.title: Suggested use (Melody-Matching)
docs.text:
Chord type Ideal when melody is Minor 7 11th Major 7 #11th

The voicing is designed so the melody note is already built into the top of the right hand — just play the voicing and the chord + melody sound together.

docs.title: Sawn-off variation (for smaller hands)
docs.text:
If you can't stretch a 9th, cut off the top note of the left hand: - LH: root + 5th only (1 – 5) - RH: 9th – 3rd – 7th (9 / 3 7) - Full: (1 5 / 9 3 7) — e.g., C minor 7: LH = C G, RH = D Eb Bb

This works best when the melody is the 7th (Bb for C minor 7).

docs.title: In the voicing system
docs.text: The Kenny Barron voicing is the most physically demanding of the five types. See Chord Voicing Principles for how it fits in, and Melody-Matching for when to reach for it.

docs.title: Melody-Matching
docs.text: Julian Bradley's technique for choosing which chord voicing to play when reading a lead sheet. The core idea: pick a voicing whose top note is the melody note. This way, playing the chord and playing the melody become one action.

docs.title: The three steps
docs.text:
1. Read the chord symbol → identify the chord type (maj7, min7, V7, etc.)
  2. Read the melody note → identify its scale degree relative to the chord (is it the 3rd? the 9th? the 7th?)
  3. Choose a voicing for that chord type that has the melody note as its top note

docs.title: Quick reference table
docs.text: Melody degree Good voicing choice Top note 3rd Shell Voicings position A (1 7 3) or Open Voicings 1 5 7 3 3rd 5th Open Voicings 1 7 3 5 or Rootless Voicings position B (7 9 3 5) 5th 7th Shell Voicings position B (1 3 7) or Open Voicings 1 5 3 7 7th 9th Rootless Voicings position A (3 5 7 9) or Fourth Voicings sawn-off (1 7 / 3 6 9) 9th 11th Kenny Barron Voicing for min7 (1 5 9 / 3 7 11) 11th #11th Kenny Barron Voicing for maj7 (1 5 9 / 3 7 #11) #11th

docs.title: Practical note
docs.text:
You only need to look at the first melody note for each chord. Most melodies move after the chord has sounded — hold the voicing down and let the melody continue above (or release the top notes if the melody dips back into the voicing range).

As you play through a standard this way, you'll naturally use a mix of voicing types — shells, open, rootless, 4ths — which creates variety and interest in the arrangement.

docs.title: Example: 'Misty' (first bars)
docs.text: Chord Melody note Degree Voicing used Ebmaj7 Bb 5th Open voicing (1 7 3 5) Bbm7 Ab 7th Shell position B (1 3 7) Eb7 Bb 5th Rootless position B Abmaj7 C 3rd Open voicing (1 5 7 3)

docs.title: Why it works
docs.text:
The chord voicing already sounds good on its own. By matching the melody note to the top of the voicing, you're not adding an untested note on top — the melody note is already proven to sound good as part of the voicing.

See Chord Voicing Principles for the underlying theory, and all five voicing type pages for the full palette.

docs.title: Open Voicings
docs.text: Four-note voicings built from the chord tones (1 3 5 7), but with the notes spread wide across bass, mid, and treble instead of stacked in order. Requires two hands. Sounds clean and spacious compared to Shell Voicings (which sit within less than an octave).

docs.title: The three open voicings (demonstrated on C major 7)
docs.text:
Choose which one to use based on the melody note — use the voicing that has the melody note as its top note:

Melody note Voicing (bottom → top) Notes in C 7th 1 5 3 7 C G E B 3rd 1 5 7 3 C G B E 5th 1 7 3 5 C B E G

docs.title: Adapting to other chord types
docs.text:
Adjust the 3rd and 7th to match the chord type — the structure stays the same:

Chord Voicing A (top = 7th) Voicing B (top = 3rd) Voicing C (top = 5th) C major 7 C G E B C G B E C B E G C minor 7 C G Eb Bb C G Bb Eb C Bb Eb G C7 C G E Bb C G Bb E C Bb E G Cø (half-dim) C Gb Eb Bb C Gb Bb Eb C Bb Eb Gb

docs.title: Suggested use
docs.text:
Open voicings work best when: - The melody note is a chord tone (1, 3, 5, or 7) - You want a clean, resonant sound with some bass weight - Contrast against shell voicings in an arrangement (shells = minimal, open = full)

They can be transposed up or down an octave — use your ear to decide which register sounds best.

docs.title: In the voicing system
docs.text: Open voicings sit between shells and rootless in complexity. For the full picture see Chord Voicing Principles. For choosing between voicings live, see Melody-Matching.

docs.title: Rootless Voicings
docs.text: Julian Bradley's most-used jazz piano voicing. Rootless voicings omit the root and are built upward from the chord's 3rd — this allows you to reach the 9th, adding a colorful, sophisticated sound.

docs.title: Why rootless?
docs.text:
• Designed for playing with a bass player: the bassist handles the root, freeing both your hands for melody and harmony
  • For solo piano: play the root in your left hand as a bass note, rootless voicing in your right hand
  • Rootless voicings are stacks of 3rds starting from the 3rd: 3 – 5 – 7 – 9

docs.title: Two positions (like Shell Voicings)
docs.text:
Position A — built from the 3rd upward:

Chord Notes (bottom → top) Degrees C major 7 E G B D 3 5 7 9 C minor 7 Eb G Bb D 3 5 7 9 C7 E G Bb D 3 5 7 9

Shortcut for C major 7: Find the major 3rd (E), then play a minor 7 chord from that note (E minor 7 = E G B D). Shortcut for C minor 7: Find the minor 3rd (Eb), then play a major 7 chord from that note (Eb major 7 = Eb G Bb D).

Special case for V7 chords: Replace the 5th with the 6th (13th):

Chord Standard pos A V7 variation C7 E G Bb D (3 5 7 9) E A Bb D (3 6 7 9)

Position B — Position A in 2nd inversion (drop the top two notes down an octave):

Chord Notes (bottom → top) Degrees C major 7 B D E G 7 9 3 5 C minor 7 Bb D Eb G 7 9 3 5 C7 Bb D E A 7 9 3 6

Position B is the same four notes as Position A — just inverted so 7 and 9 are at the bottom.

docs.title: Suggested use (Melody-Matching)
docs.text:
Melody note Use 9th Position A (top note = 9th) 5th Position B (top note = 5th)

For solo piano: root in left hand + rootless voicing in right hand = melody note on top. For band: rootless voicing in left hand, melody or solo in right hand.

docs.title: Lead sheet note
docs.text: Most lead sheets write chords as 7th chords (Cm7, G7, etc.) even when extensions are implied. You can freely add the 9th to any 7th chord — you don't need the sheet to say "Cm9" to play the 9th.

docs.title: In the voicing system
docs.text: Rootless voicings are the core intermediate voicing. See Chord Voicing Principles for context, Open Voicings for the simpler alternative, and Melody-Matching for how to select between them.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
Welcome to your 1-Year Jazz Piano Practice Plan! This structured plan is designed to help you develop foundational skills in jazz piano by focusing on key areas of practice, referred to as the Five Pillars. Each month, you will explore a new major key and its relative minor, ensuring comprehensive growth in your jazz piano journey.

The notes follows this guide. Other material can be found in the following video.

docs.title: 1. Monthly Key Focus
docs.text: Each month, you will focus on one major key and its relative minor. This allows you to: - Gain confidence in all 12 keys. - Build muscle memory for scales, chords, and improvisation patterns.

docs.title: 2. The Five Pillars
docs.text: The plan is divided into five main areas of practice: 1. Pillar - Scales: Develop finger dexterity and familiarity with the keyboard. 2. Pillar - Chords: Learn the building blocks of jazz harmony. 3. Pillar - Voicings: Create jazz-style harmonizations. 4. Pillar - Lead Sheets: Combine melody and harmony to play complete songs. 5. Pillar - Improvisation: Experiment with creating your own melodies.

docs.title: 3. Difficulty
docs.text:
Each section of this plan will vary in difficulty based on your experience and progress. To ensure you focus on the right material for your skill level, reference the difficulty levels below. You can access detailed documents for each difficulty level:

  • Beginner difficulty: Aimed at those new to music or transitioning from basic to intermediate skills.
  • Intermediate difficulty: For players who have a good understanding of music theory, scales, and basic improvisation.
  • [[Advanced plan|Advanced difficulty]]: For experienced musicians looking to master advanced techniques and complex harmonic concepts.

docs.title: Benefits of This Plan
docs.text: By following this practice plan, you will: - Master the foundational elements of jazz piano. - Improve your ability to play jazz standards fluently. - Develop improvisational skills for creative expression. - Gain confidence in reading and interpreting lead sheets. - Build a strong technical foundation for advanced jazz concepts.

docs.title: How to Use This Plan
docs.text:
1. Daily Practice: Dedicate time to each of the five pillars every day. Even 20-30 minutes per day can lead to significant progress.
  2. Sequential Progression: Move through the keys in the order provided, ensuring a balanced and thorough learning experience.
  3. Focus on Consistency: Aim for steady, consistent practice rather than long, irregular sessions.

docs.title: What You’ll Need
docs.text:
• A piano or keyboard with at least 61 keys.
  • A metronome (physical or digital).
  • Beginner-friendly lead sheets for jazz standards like “Autumn Leaves.”
  • A positive attitude and patience with yourself as you learn!

docs.title: Next Steps
docs.text:
1. Start with the file for the Key: C Major & A Minor, which includes beginner exercises for this key.
  2. Refer to the Pillar Files for a deeper understanding of each practice area.

Enjoy the process, and let the music guide you! 🎵

docs.title: Beginner Plan
docs.text: The goal of this plan is to help you build a solid foundation in music theory, technique, and improvisation. As a beginner, you'll focus on basic concepts and gradually build your skills across all five pillars.

docs.title: 1. Monthly Key Focus
docs.text:
Each month, you'll focus on one major key and its relative minor. This will help you:

  • Learn the basic scales in each key.
  • Familiarize yourself with the major and minor chords in those keys.
  • Build muscle memory and confidence in all 12 keys.

docs.title: Example:
docs.text:
• Month 1: C major & A minor
  • Month 2: G major & E minor
  • Month 3: D major & B minor

docs.title: 1. Pillar - Scales
docs.text:
• Goal: Develop basic finger dexterity and understanding of major and minor scales.
  • Tasks:
  • Practice 5-finger positions for each key.
  • Play simple major and minor scales hands separately.
  • Begin practicing scales hands together at a slow tempo.
  • Beginner scales exercises

docs.title: 2. Pillar - Chords
docs.text:
• Goal: Learn the basic building blocks of harmony and chord progressions.
  • Tasks:
  • Learn major and minor triads in all keys.
  • Practice chord inversions.
  • Start working on basic progressions like I-IV-V (C-F-G in C major).
  • Beginner chords exercises

docs.title: 3. Pillar - Voicings
docs.text:
• Goal: Start building simple chord voicings.
  • Tasks:
  • Learn basic triad voicings (root position, first inversion, second inversion).
  • Experiment with adding simple voicings to your chord progressions (I-IV-V).
  • Play chords in different inversions.
  • Beginner voicings exercises

docs.title: 4. Pillar - Lead Sheets
docs.text:
• Goal: Combine melody and harmony to play simple songs.
  • Tasks:
  • Start playing basic lead sheets that feature simple melodies and chord symbols.
  • Focus on understanding how the melody fits with the harmony.
  • Practice reading chord symbols and playing the appropriate chords.
  • Beginner lead sheets exercises

docs.title: 5. Pillar - Improvisation
docs.text:
• Goal: Begin experimenting with creating your own simple melodies.
  • Tasks:
  • Improvise using the major and minor scales you've learned.
  • Focus on basic rhythmic patterns and note choices.
  • Use chord progressions like I-IV-V as a foundation for your improvisation.
  • Beginner improvisation exercises

docs.title: 3. Weekly Practice Breakdown
docs.text:
• Monday: Scale practice (hands separately, then hands together)
  • Tuesday: Chord practice (triads and inversions)
  • Wednesday: Lead sheets (simple songs, focus on melody and harmony)
  • Thursday: Voicings practice (chord inversions and voicings)
  • Friday: Improvisation (improv over basic progressions)
  • Saturday: Review and practice any challenging areas
  • Sunday: Rest or light practice (review scales and chords)

docs.title: 4. Progress Tracking
docs.text:
Keep track of your progress each week:

  • Are you able to play your scales at a steady tempo?
  • Can you play major and minor triads in all keys?
  • Are you comfortable reading lead sheets with basic chord symbols?

By the end of the first few months, you should be able to confidently play basic scales, chords, and lead sheets, and begin experimenting with simple improvisation. This plan is meant to give you the foundation for more advanced concepts down the road. Stay consistent and patient with your practice!

docs.title: Tips for Practicing Chords
docs.text:
• Focus on smooth, clean transitions between chords.
  • Start slow and work up to speed while maintaining clarity.
  • Practice both hands separately before playing together.
  • Experiment with different progressions to develop flexibility.
  • Use a metronome to practice chord changes in time with the beat.

docs.title: Major and minor triads with inversions
Tips for Practicing Improvisation
docs.text:
• Start Slow: Begin by improvising slowly and focusing on using simple, clear ideas.
  • Record Yourself: Recording your improvisations allows you to listen back and analyze your phrasing and development.
  • Improvise Every Day: Try to improvise every day, even if it's just for a few minutes. The more you practice, the more confident and fluent you'll become.
  • Play Along with Backing Tracks: Use backing tracks or jam along with other musicians to simulate real-life improvisational situations.

docs.title: Tips for Practicing Lead Sheets
docs.text:
• Start with simpler songs and work your way up to more complex jazz standards.
  • Focus on playing the chords in a rhythmically consistent way, using different patterns for variation.
  • Practice improvising over the chord changes to develop your ability to fill in harmonic gaps.
  • Record yourself playing from lead sheets to track your progress and identify areas for improvement.

docs.title: Tips for Practicing Scales
docs.text:
1. Use a metronome for consistent timing.
  2. Start at a slow tempo, gradually increasing speed.
  3. Focus on even tone and rhythm across all fingers.
  4. Divide practice:
  5. Play hands separately first, then together.
  6. Alternate between rhythms and triads.

Mastering scales ensures a solid technical base, facilitating your growth as a jazz pianist.

docs.title: 1. Building the Scales
docs.text:
• Monthly Focus: Each month emphasizes one major key and its minor key (not necessarily the relative minor).

  • Scale Formulas:

  • Major Scale Formula: Whole-Whole-Half-Whole-Whole-Whole-Half (W-W-H-W-W-W-H).

  • Natural Minor Scale Formula: Whole-Half-Whole-Whole-Half-Whole-Whole (W-H-W-W-H-W-W).

  • Chromatic Scale: Includes all 12 semitones (half-steps).

  • Diatonic Scale: Any heptatonic scale with 5 whole steps and 2 half-steps.

  • Fingering Techniques: Proper fingering is emphasized to ensure smooth and consistent play.

docs.title: 2. Playing Techniques
docs.text:
To enhance rhythm, fluency, and harmony:

  • Charleston Rhythm: A syncopated rhythm (1-2-&-3-4-&) introduces swing to your scales.

  • Diatonic Triads: Play triads derived from the scale to build understanding of harmony.

  • Hands Together: Practice scales with both hands in unison to develop coordination.

  • Two Octaves: Expand scale practice across two octaves to improve flexibility.

docs.title: Tips for Practicing Voicings
docs.text:
• Start with basic voicings and gradually add complexity.
  • Pay attention to the distance between the notes of the voicing to ensure clarity.
  • Practice both closed and open voicings to understand different textural effects.
  • Focus on smooth transitions between chords by prioritizing voice leading.
  • Experiment with different inversions and drops to find your preferred sound.

docs.title: Intermediate Plan
docs.text: This plan is designed for musicians who have a basic understanding of scales, chords, and simple songs. At this level, you'll deepen your knowledge of music theory, develop technical skills, and expand your improvisation abilities. The focus will be on enhancing your playing, learning more complex progressions, and incorporating more advanced concepts into your improvisation.

docs.title: 1. Monthly Key Focus
docs.text:
Each month, you'll focus on a new major key and its relative minor, along with key signatures and modes. This will help you:

  • Learn more advanced scale patterns, including modes.
  • Deepen your understanding of chord progressions and harmony.
  • Become comfortable with more complex rhythms and chord changes.

docs.title: Example:
docs.text:
• Month 1: C major & A minor, explore modes (Ionian, Dorian, Phrygian)
  • Month 2: G major & E minor, explore secondary dominants
  • Month 3: D major & B minor, work on diminished and augmented chords

docs.title: 1. Pillar - Scales
docs.text:
• Goal: Expand your scale knowledge to include modes and other scale types.
  • Tasks:
  • Learn modes (Dorian, Phrygian, Lydian, Mixolydian) in all keys.
  • Practice scale patterns in both hands, focusing on smooth transitions.
  • Start practicing two-octave scales and arpeggios for each mode.

docs.title: 2. Pillar - Chords
docs.text:
• Goal: Deepen your knowledge of harmony with more complex chords and progressions.
  • Tasks:
  • Learn seventh chords (major, minor, dominant) and inversions.
  • Start learning extended chords (9ths, 11ths, 13ths).
  • Practice common chord progressions, including ii-V-I in various keys.

docs.title: 3. Pillar - Voicings
docs.text:
• Goal: Create more jazz-style harmonizations and chord voicings.
  • Tasks:
  • Practice rootless voicings (especially for dominant seventh chords).
  • Experiment with drop 2 and drop 3 voicings.
  • Practice voice leading techniques to create smoother transitions between chords.

docs.title: 4. Pillar - Lead Sheets
docs.text:
• Goal: Enhance your ability to play more complex lead sheets with different harmonies.
  • Tasks:
  • Practice lead sheets with more complex chord progressions and inversions.
  • Learn to play both the melody and harmony while reading the sheet music.
  • Focus on reading more complex rhythms and syncopations.

docs.title: 5. Pillar - Improvisation
docs.text:
• Goal: Expand your improvisation skills by incorporating more advanced techniques and harmonic knowledge.
  • Tasks:
  • Improvise using modes and seventh chords.
  • Start incorporating chromaticism and passing tones in your improvisation.
  • Improvise over more complex progressions (ii-V-I, iii-vi-ii-V).
  • Focus on rhythmic variety, experimenting with different subdivisions of beats.

docs.title: 3. Weekly Practice Breakdown
docs.text:
• Monday: Scales and arpeggios (hands together, two octaves)
  • Tuesday: Chord practice (seventh chords, inversions, and extended chords)
  • Wednesday: Lead sheets (focus on more complex harmonies and rhythms)
  • Thursday: Voicings (rootless voicings, drop 2, and drop 3 voicings)
  • Friday: Improvisation (over ii-V-I progressions and modal improvisation)
  • Saturday: Review and practice challenging areas (focus on weaker skills)
  • Sunday: Rest or light practice (review scales and chord progressions)

docs.title: 4. Progress Tracking
docs.text:
Monitor your progress each week:

  • Are you able to play scales and arpeggios fluently across multiple octaves?
  • Can you play seventh chords and extended chords in all keys?
  • Are you able to read and perform more complex lead sheets?
  • How comfortable are you improvising with different chord progressions and modes?

By the end of this plan, you should be able to confidently play and improvise over more complex chord progressions and modal scales, and understand a broader range of harmony and voice leading. This intermediate plan will build the foundation for even more advanced jazz theory and performance skills in the future. Keep practicing consistently to improve your technique and musicality!

docs.title: The Chord Symbol Guide — Julian Bradley
docs.text: Format: PDF reference sheet (1 page) Author/Teacher: Julian Bradley (Jazz Tutorial) Core focus: Complete reference for jazz chord symbol notation used in lead sheets

docs.title: Key takeaways
docs.text:
• Six 7th chord types matter in jazz: maj7, min7, V7, minor-major 7, half-diminished, diminished 7
  • 6th chords: 6th replaces the 7th — Cm6 uses a major 6th (A natural), not a minor 6th (Ab)
  • All extensions (9, 11, 13) are built from the major scale; altered extensions (b9, #9, #11, b13) appear only on V7 chords
  • Notation convention: use #4 for maj7 chords, 11 for min7/V7 (not interchangeable)
  • Sus4 replaces the 3rd; 11th chord adds 4th alongside the 3rd — different sounds
  • C7b13 implies the full C altered scale (C Db Eb E Gb Ab Bb)
  • Slash chords: chord/bass note — right hand plays chord, left hand plays bass note
  • C7(#9) = "The James Bond Chord"

docs.title: New concepts introduced
docs.text:
• Chord Symbols — full reference page for all jazz chord symbol types and notations
  • Altered Dominants — b9, #9, #11, b13, C7 altered and the altered scale

docs.title: Contradictions / tensions
docs.text: • None with existing wiki content.

docs.title: Pages updated
docs.text:
• Pillar - Lead Sheets — added chord symbol reference, slash chords, link to Chord Symbols page
  • Pillar - Chords — added minor-major 7, half-diminished, diminished 7 chord types
  • Julian Bradley — source added

docs.title: The Chord Voicing Guide — Julian Bradley
docs.text: Format: PDF ebook (11 pages) Author/Teacher: Julian Bradley (Jazz Tutorial) Core focus: Five types of jazz piano chord voicings, how to choose between them, and how to practice them

docs.title: Key takeaways
docs.text:
• Every jazz chord can be voiced many ways; 5 professional-level types are covered
  • Three voicing principles apply to all types: no repeated notes, root/3rd/7th at the bottom, leave space for the melody
  • Voicings are demonstrated on the three main 7th chord types (maj7, min7, V7) — all others are adaptations
  • Melody-Matching is Julian's technique for choosing which voicing to use: pick the voicing whose top note matches the current melody note
  • Practice one voicing type at a time via side-slipping (transpose around all 12 keys before moving on)
  • Count interval patterns, not note names, when memorizing voicings

docs.title: New concepts introduced
docs.text:
• Chord Voicing Principles — 3 universal principles all voicings follow
  • Open Voicings — 4-note spread voicings (1 5 3 7, 1 5 7 3, 1 7 3 5)
  • Rootless Voicings — Julian's most-used; built from 3rd (3 5 7 9), two positions
  • Kenny Barron Voicing — stacks of 5ths, two-handed, requires 9th stretch
  • Fourth Voicings — chords built in 4ths from the 7th (7 3 6 / 9 5 1)
  • Melody-Matching — technique for selecting which voicing fits the melody

docs.title: Contradictions / tensions
docs.text: • None with existing wiki content. The existing Shell Voicings page covers shells well; this guide adds Position A/B framing and situates shells within the full 5-type system.

docs.title: Pages updated
docs.text:
• Shell Voicings — added Position A/B framing, suggested uses, link to full voicing system
  • Pillar - Voicings — updated to reference all 5 voicing types with links

docs.title: Scales
docs.text: The A major and A minor scales form the foundation of this course. Understanding their structure and how they relate to modes is essential to jazz harmony.

docs.title: A Major Scale
docs.text: A A♯ B C C♯ D D♯ E F F♯ G G♯ A - The A major scale is the foundation of Ionian (major mode) and also serves as the starting point for Lydian and other scales. - The key features include a natural major 7th and a whole-step between most notes.

docs.title: A Minor Scales
docs.text:
• A Natural Minor (Aeolian): A A♯ B C C♯ D D♯ E F F♯ G G♯ A

  • This scale is based on the Aeolian mode and has a natural minor sound with a flat 3rd, 6th, and 7th.

  • A Harmonic Minor: A A♯ B C C♯ D D♯ E F F♯ G G♯ A

  • This scale has a raised 7th, creating a leading tone that resolves to the tonic, with a characteristic exotic sound.

  • A Melodic Minor (Jazz): A A♯ B C C♯ D D♯ E F F♯ G G♯ A

  • The melodic minor scale, in jazz, is played with a raised 6th and 7th ascending, and the natural 6th and 7th descending.

docs.text: Understanding chords in both the major and minor keys will provide the harmonic foundation for the improvisation and composition.

docs.title: Voicings
docs.text: This pillar explores how to play the chords in a musically effective and harmonically clear way, including different voicings such as root position, inversions, and shell voicings.

docs.title: Major and Minor Voicings
docs.text:
• For A major, start by using basic triads (A C♯ E), and later expand into seventh chords (Amaj7).
  • In A minor, use triads (A C E) and seventh chords (Am7) in root positions and inversions.

docs.title: Open and Closed Voicings
docs.text:
• Closed voicings place the notes of a chord as close together as possible, creating a tight sound.
  • Open voicings spread the notes across octaves to create a more open, airy sound.

docs.title: Shell Voicings
docs.text:
Shell voicings are an essential component of jazz harmony, where you play only the most essential notes of a chord: the root, third, and seventh. This simplified voicing provides the harmonic context without the clutter of the full chord.

  • Amaj7 (A major): A (root), C♯ (third), G♯ (seventh)
  • Am7 (A minor): A (root), C (third), G (seventh)
  • Bm7 (B minor): B (root), D (third), A (seventh)
  • G7 (G dominant): G (root), B (third), F (seventh)

Shell voicings are often used in comping and can be played in both closed voicing (notes close together) or open voicing (spread across octaves) to create different textures. These voicings work well when accompanying soloists or for playing rhythmically in an ensemble.

docs.title: Improvisation
docs.text: Improvisation over the A major and A minor keys can be approached using scales, arpeggios, and chord tones.

docs.title: For A Major:
docs.text:
• Modes: Ionian (A major), Lydian (D major).
  • Use the A major scale for melodic improvisation, focusing on chord tones (A, C♯, E, G♯) for Amaj7, and using appropriate tension notes like the 7th and 9th.

docs.title: For A Minor:
docs.text:
• Natural Minor: Use the A minor scale, focusing on resolving to the tonic.
  • Harmonic Minor: Emphasize the raised 7th (G♯) in improvisation, which creates a strong tension-resolution feel.
  • Melodic Minor: Use ascending melodic minor to incorporate the 6th and 7th scale degrees in your improvisation.

docs.title: Open and Closed Voicings

  • Open voicings spread the notes across octaves to create a more open, airy sound.

docs.title: Shell Voicings

Shell voicings are an essential component of jazz harmony, where you play only the most essential notes of a chord: the root, third, and seventh. This simplified voicing provides the harmonic context without the clutter of the full chord.

  • B♭maj7 (B♭ major): B♭ (root), D (third), A (seventh)
  • B♭m7 (B♭ minor): B♭ (root), D♭ (third), A♭ (seventh)
  • Cm7 (C minor): C (root), E♭ (third), B♭ (seventh)
  • F7 (F dominant): F (root), A (third), E♭ (seventh)

docs.title: Lead Sheets

docs.title: Key Points:
docs.text:
• Chord symbols indicate which notes to play and often include alterations, extensions (like 7ths, 9ths), or specific modes (such as "B♭7" for dominant 7th).
  • Reading and interpreting these symbols in context is critical for jazz performance.

  • Melodic Minor: Use ascending melodic minor to incorporate the 6th and 7th scale degrees in your improvisation.

• For B major, start by using basic triads (B D♯ F♯), and later expand into seventh chords (Bmaj7).
  • In B minor, use triads (B D♯ F♯) and seventh chords (Bm7) in root positions and inversions.

docs.title: Open and Closed Voicings
docs.text:
• Closed voicings place the notes of a chord as close together as possible, creating a tight sound.
  • Open voicings spread the notes across octaves to create a more open, airy sound.

docs.title: Shell Voicings
docs.text:
Shell voicings are an essential component of jazz harmony, where you play only the most essential notes of a chord: the root, third, and seventh. This simplified voicing provides the harmonic context without the clutter of the full chord.

  • Bmaj7 (B major): B (root), D♯ (third), A♯ (seventh)
  • Bm7 (B minor): B (root), D♯ (third), A (seventh)
  • C♯m7 (C♯ minor): C♯ (root), E (third), B (seventh)
  • F♯7 (F♯ dominant): F♯ (root), A♯ (third), E (seventh)
  • G♯m7 (G♯ minor): G♯ (root), B (third), F♯ (seventh)

Shell voicings are often used in comping and can be played in both closed voicing (notes close together) or open voicing (spread across octaves) to create different textures. These voicings work well when accompanying soloists or for playing rhythmically in an ensemble.

docs.title: Lead Sheets
docs.text: Lead sheets will help you interpret the harmonies and structure of a song. In B major and minor, you will be able to read chord symbols like "Bmaj7", "Bm", "G♯7", and apply the relevant chord voicings.

docs.title: Key Points:
docs.text:
• Chord symbols indicate which notes to play and often include alterations, extensions (like 7ths, 9ths), or specific modes (such as "B7" for dominant 7th).
  • Reading and interpreting these symbols in context is critical for jazz performance.

docs.title: Improvisation
docs.text: Improvisation over the B major and minor keys can be approached using scales, arpeggios, and chord tones.

docs.title: For B Major:
docs.text:
• Modes: Ionian (B major), Lydian (E major).
  • Use the B major scale for melodic improvisation, focusing on chord tones (B, D♯, F♯, A♯) for Bmaj7, and using appropriate tension notes like the 7th and 9th.

docs.title: For B Minor:
docs.text:
• Natural Minor: Use the B minor scale, focusing on resolving to the tonic.
  • Harmonic Minor: Emphasize the raised 7th (A♯) in improvisation, which creates a strong tension-resolution feel.
  • Melodic Minor: Use ascending melodic minor to incorporate the 6th and 7th scale degrees in your improvisation.

docs.text: C D E F G A B C - The C major scale is the foundation of Ionian (major mode) and also serves as the starting point for C Lydian and other scales. - The key features include a natural major 7th and a whole-step between most notes.

docs.title: C Minor Scales
docs.text:
• C Natural Minor (Aeolian): C D♭ E♭ F G♭ A♭ B♭ C

  • C Melodic Minor (Jazz): C D E♭ F G A B C

  • The melodic minor scale, in jazz, is played with a raised 6th and 7th ascending, and the natural 6th and 7th descending.

docs.title: Shell Voicings
docs.text:
Shell voicings are an essential component of jazz harmony, where you play only the most essential notes of a chord: the root, third, and seventh. This simplified voicing provides the harmonic context without the clutter of the full chord.

  • D#maj7 (D# major): D# (root), F# (third), A (seventh)
  • D#m7 (D# minor): D# (root), F# (third), B (seventh)

Shell voicings are often used in comping and can be played in both closed voicing (notes close together) or open voicing (spread across octaves) to create different textures. These voicings work well when accompanying soloists or for playing rhythmically in an ensemble.

docs.title: Voicings
docs.text: This pillar explores how to play the chords in a musically effective and harmonically clear way, including different voicings such as root position, inversions, and shell voicings.

docs.title: Major and Minor Voicings
docs.text:
• For G major, start by using basic triads (G B D), and later expand into seventh chords (Gmaj7).
  • In G minor, use triads (G Bb D) and seventh chords (Gm7) in root positions and inversions.

docs.title: Open and Closed Voicings
docs.text:
• Closed voicings place the notes of a chord as close together as possible, creating a tight sound.
  • Open voicings spread the notes across octaves to create a more open, airy sound.

docs.title: Shell Voicings
docs.text:
Shell voicings are an essential component of jazz harmony, where you play only the most essential notes of a chord: the root, third, and seventh. This simplified voicing provides the harmonic context without the clutter of the full chord.

  • Gmaj7 (G major): G (root), B (third), D (seventh)
  • Gm7 (G minor): G (root), Bb (third), D (seventh)
  • D7 (D dominant): D (root), F# (third), C (seventh)
  • Cmaj7 (C major): C (root), E (third), G (seventh)

Shell voicings are often used in comping and can be played in both closed voicing (notes close together) or open voicing (spread across octaves) to create different textures. These voicings work well when accompanying soloists or for playing rhythmically in an ensemble.

docs.title: Lead Sheets
docs.text: Lead sheets will help you interpret the harmonies and structure of a song. In G major and minor, you will be able to read chord symbols like "Gmaj7", "Gm", "D7", and apply the relevant chord voicings.

docs.title: Key Points:
docs.text:
• Chord symbols indicate which notes to play and often include alterations, extensions (like 7ths, 9ths), or specific modes (such as "G7" for dominant 7th).
  • Reading and interpreting these symbols in context is critical for jazz performance.

docs.title: Improvisation
docs.text: Improvisation over the G major and minor keys can be approached using scales, arpeggios, and chord tones.

docs.title: For G Major:
docs.text:
• Modes: Ionian (G major), Lydian (D major).
  • Use the G major scale for melodic improvisation, focusing on chord tones (G, B, D, F#) for Gmaj7, and using appropriate tension notes like the 7th and 9th.

docs.title: For G Minor:
docs.text:
• Natural Minor: Use the G minor scale, focusing on resolving to the tonic.
  • Harmonic Minor: Emphasize the raised 7th (F#) in improvisation, which creates a strong tension-resolution feel.
  • Melodic Minor: Use ascending melodic minor to incorporate the 6th and 7th scale degrees in your improvisation.

docs.title: Scales
docs.text: The Ab major and Ab minor scales provide the harmonic foundation for both Ionian (major) and Aeolian (minor) modes, and their related scales, such as harmonic and melodic minor.

docs.title: Ab Major Scale
docs.text:
Ab Bb C D Eb F G Ab

  • The Ab major scale is based on the Ionian mode (major scale), and it is characterized by its whole-step intervals and a natural major 7th.

docs.title: Ab Minor Scales
docs.text:
• Ab Natural Minor (Aeolian): Ab Bb Cb (B) Db Eb Fb (E) Ab

  • This is the natural minor scale, with flat 3rd, 6th, and 7th intervals, creating a sad or somber sound.

  • Ab Harmonic Minor: Ab Bb Cb (B) Db Eb F (F#) Ab

  • The harmonic minor scale has a raised 7th, creating a more dramatic tension that resolves to the tonic.

  • Ab Melodic Minor (Jazz): Ab Bb Cb (B) Db Eb F (F#) Ab

  • This ascending melodic minor scale raises the 6th and 7th degrees, which adds complexity to melodic improvisation, with the 6th and 7th returning to the natural minor scale in descending passages.

docs.title: Voicings
docs.text: Voicings are key for playing chords effectively in jazz. They range from simple triads to more complex seventh and extended chord structures.

docs.title: Major and Minor Voicings
docs.text:
• For G# major, start with basic triads (G# B D#) and expand into seventh chords (G#maj7).
  • For G# minor, use triads (G# B D#) and seventh chords (G#m7) in root positions and inversions.

docs.title: Open and Closed Voicings
docs.text:
• Closed voicings are used for more compact sound, where the notes are close together.
  • Open voicings spread the notes out, creating a fuller, more spacious sound.

docs.title: Shell Voicings
docs.text:
Shell voicings are simplified chords that consist of the most important notes: the root, third, and seventh.

  • G#maj7: G# (root), B (third), D# (seventh)
  • G#m7: G# (root), B (third), D# (seventh)
  • D#7: D# (root), F## (third), C# (seventh)
  • C#maj7: C# (root), E (third), G# (seventh)

docs.title: Lead Sheets
docs.text: Lead sheets help you interpret the harmonic progressions of a song. When reading lead sheets in G# major or minor, you’ll see chord symbols like "G#maj7", "G#m", "D#7", which indicate the chords you need to play, and often, the modes (like "G#m7") or alterations (such as "F##" for a sharped note).

docs.title: Improvisation
docs.text: For improvisation over the G# major and G# minor keys, focus on scales, arpeggios, and chord tones.

docs.title: G♯-A♭ Major:
docs.text:
• Ionian Mode (G# major): Use the G# major scale, focusing on chord tones (G#, B, D#) for smooth improvisation. Utilize the 7th (F##) and 9th (A#) for added color.
  • Lydian Mode (D# major): The Lydian mode emphasizes the sharp 4th (D#), giving it a more suspended, unresolved feel.

docs.title: G♯-A♭ Minor:
docs.text:
• Natural Minor (Aeolian): Focus on the tonic (G#) and the flat 3rd, 6th, and 7th for a melancholic, darker sound.
  • Harmonic Minor: Emphasize the raised 7th (F##) to create tension that resolves back to the tonic.
  • Melodic Minor: Focus on the raised 6th and 7th (F##, A#) in your ascending improvisation, and the natural 6th and 7th (F#, A#) when descending.

docs.title: Cadence
docs.text:
A cadence is a progression of at least two chords that signals the end of a musical phrase, section, or piece. It creates a sense of resolution or continuation. Understanding cadences is essential for interpreting musical structure and harmony.

There are two main types of cadences: finished and unfinished. Finished cadences bring a sense of closure, while unfinished cadences leave the music open-ended, creating a feeling of anticipation.

docs.title: Finished Cadences
docs.text: Finished cadences resolve the harmonic tension and end on chord I (the tonic chord), providing a sense of stability and closure.

docs.title: 1. Perfect (Authentic) Cadence
docs.text:
• Progression: V – I
  • Description: This is the most conclusive cadence, providing a strong sense of finality. It is often used at the end of a piece or section, marking a full resolution.

docs.title: 2. Plagal Cadence ("Amen" Cadence)
docs.text:
• Progression: IV – I
  • Description: This cadence is commonly referred to as the "Amen" cadence due to its frequent use at the end of hymns. It has a softer resolution compared to the authentic cadence.

docs.title: Unfinished Cadences
docs.text: Unfinished cadences do not resolve to chord I, leaving the phrase open-ended and creating a sense of tension or anticipation.

docs.title: 1. Imperfect (Half) Cadence
docs.text:
• Progression: I/II/IV – V
  • Description: This cadence ends on V, leaving the phrase unresolved and creating an expectation of continuation. It is commonly used as a lead-in to the next phrase or section.

docs.title: 2. Interrupted (Deceptive) Cadence
docs.text:
• Progression: V – VI
  • Description: This cadence creates the expectation of a perfect cadence (V – I) but surprises the listener by resolving to the VI chord instead. It interrupts the expected resolution, creating a deceptive ending.

docs.title: How Cadences Relate to Scales
docs.text:
Cadences play a crucial role in harmonic structure and can guide your scale practice. Knowing how different cadences resolve within a key can help you navigate chord progressions and improve your ability to anticipate harmonic changes in your playing or composition.

By understanding these cadences, you'll gain a deeper insight into how musical phrases function and how you can use scales to improvise and build harmonic progressions.

docs.title: Intervals
docs.text:
Difference in pitch between two sounds

  • harmonic or vertical: The two notes sound simultaneously
  • melodic or horizontal: The two notes sound successively

docs.title: Inversions
docs.text:
1. The interval number and the number of its inversion always add up to nine
  2. The inversion of a major interval is a minor interval, and vice versa.
  3. The inversion of a perfect interval is also perfect.
  4. The inversion of an augmented interval is a diminished interval and vice versa.

docs.title: Modes of the Major Scale
docs.text: The modes are seven different scales derived from the major scale, each starting from a different degree of the scale. They offer unique sounds and are foundational to understanding harmony and melody in Western music.

docs.title: Ionian (I) – Major Scale
docs.text:
The Ionian mode is simply the major scale. It is the most commonly used scale in Western music.

notes  1 - 2 - 3 4 - 5 - 6 - 7 1
chords M - m - m M - M - m - ° M

docs.title: Characteristics
docs.text:
• It is the most stable and resolved of all the modes.
  • Often used to express a happy, bright sound.
  • The tonic (I) provides a strong resolution in cadences.

docs.title: Dorian (II) – Minor Scale with a Natural 6
docs.text:
The Dorian mode is a minor scale with a raised 6th degree. It blends the characteristics of both major and minor scales.

notes  1 - 2 3 - 4 - 5 - 6 7 - 1
chords m - m M - M - m - ° M - m

docs.title: Characteristics
docs.text:
• The natural 6th gives it a less somber and more open sound compared to the Aeolian mode.
  • Commonly used in jazz, blues, and folk music.
  • Provides a minor, yet more optimistic feel.

docs.title: Phrygian (III) – Minor Scale with a Flatted 2nd
docs.text:
The Phrygian mode is a minor scale with a flattened 2nd degree, giving it a distinct, exotic flavor.

notes  1 2 - 3 - 4 - 5 6 - 7 - 1
chords m M - M - m - º M - m - m

docs.title: Characteristics
docs.text:
• The flattened 2nd degree gives it a tense, almost Spanish or Middle Eastern sound.
  • Often used in flamenco and metal music.
  • It has a darker, more mysterious feel.

docs.title: Lydian (IV) – Major Scale with a Sharped 4th
docs.text:
The Lydian mode is a major scale with a raised 4th degree, giving it a dreamy and bright sound.

notes  1 - 2 - 3 - 4 5 - 6 - 7 1
chords M - M - m - º M - m - m M

docs.title: Characteristics
docs.text:
• The raised 4th creates a floating, ethereal quality.
  • Often used in film scores and progressive rock.
  • It has an uplifting and bright sound compared to the Ionian mode.

docs.title: Mixolydian (V) – Major Scale with a Flatted 7th
docs.text:
The Mixolydian mode is a major scale with a flattened 7th degree, making it sound slightly bluesy or rock-oriented.

notes  1 - 2 - 3 4 - 5 - 6 7 - 1
chords M - m - º M - m - m M - M

docs.title: Characteristics
docs.text:
• The lowered 7th gives it a bluesy or rock feel.
  • Often used in rock, blues, and country music.
  • The flattened 7th creates a dominant seventh chord that resolves naturally to the tonic.

docs.title: Aeolian (VI) – Natural Minor Scale
docs.text:
The Aeolian mode is simply the natural minor scale. It has a darker and more somber sound than the Ionian or Dorian modes.

notes  1 - 2 3 - 4 - 5 6 - 7 - 1
chords m - º M - m - m M - M - m

docs.title: Characteristics
docs.text:
• This scale has a more melancholic or serious tone.
  • Commonly used in classical, rock, and heavy metal music.
  • It often serves as the basis for minor key pieces and creates a sense of tension that resolves to the tonic.

docs.title: Locrian (VII) – Minor Scale with Flatted 2nd, 5th, and 6th
docs.text:
The Locrian mode is a highly unstable scale, often considered the most dissonant due to its diminished fifth.

notes  1 2 - 3 - 4 5 - 6 - 7 - 1
chords º M - m - m M - M - m - m

docs.title: Characteristics
docs.text:
• The flatted 2nd, 5th, and 6th create a very tense and unstable sound.
  • It is rarely used as a key center, but it can be used to create tension in a piece.
  • Often found in jazz and experimental music where dissonance is explored.

docs.title: Conclusion
docs.text: Each mode brings its own unique flavor and emotion to music. Whether you're writing a melody or harmony, understanding these modes gives you more tools to express different moods and atmospheres. The modes can be used as a foundation for improvisation, composition, or simply to experiment with new sounds.

docs.title: Shell Voicings
docs.text: The simplest jazz piano voicing — just three notes: root, 3rd, and 7th. These are the three notes that define what kind of chord it is (maj7, min7, or V7). Shell voicings give a bare, minimal sound and are the foundation before adding more complex voicings.

docs.title: The three notes and what they do
docs.text:
• Root — defines the chord's tonal center
  • 3rd — determines major (major 3rd) or minor (minor 3rd)
  • 7th — determines quality: major 7th (maj7), minor 7th (min7 or V7)

The 5th is omitted — it adds no harmonic information and just clutters the voicing.

docs.title: Two positions (Julian Bradley)
docs.text:
The 3rd and 7th can be arranged in either order above the root:

Position Order (bottom → top) C major 7 C minor 7 C7 Position A 1 – 3 – 7 C E B C Eb Bb C E Bb Position B 1 – 7 – 3 C B E C Bb Eb C Bb E

Both positions use the same three notes — only the middle note changes.

docs.title: Suggested uses (Melody-Matching)
docs.text:
• When the melody plays the 3rd → use Position B (1 7 3) — 3rd is on top
  • When the melody plays the 7th → use Position A (1 3 7) — 7th is on top

See Melody-Matching for the full system.

docs.title: Shell voicings for the three main chord types
docs.text: Chord type Position A (1 3 7) Position B (1 7 3) C major 7 C E B C B E C minor 7 C Eb Bb C Bb Eb C7 C E Bb C Bb E

docs.title: Voice leading in ii-V-I
docs.text:
Shell voicings allow smooth voice leading — notes move by small steps between chords:

ii:  Dm7  — D F C    (root, 3rd, 7th)
V:   G7   — G B F    (root, 3rd, 7th)  — F is common tone with Dm7
I:   Cmaj7 — C E B   (root, 3rd, 7th)  — B is a half-step from C

The 3rd and 7th of each chord resolve smoothly to the 7th and 3rd of the next (Guide Tones).

docs.title: When to use shell voicings
docs.text:
• Minimal, sparse sound — creates contrast against more complex voicings
  • Songs where melody sits on 3rds and 7ths (e.g., All The Things You Are)
  • Beginner level — learn these before moving to Open Voicings, Rootless Voicings, Kenny Barron Voicing, and Fourth Voicings

Shell voicings work especially well juxtaposed with more complex voicings — the contrast is itself an arrangement tool.

docs.title: Practice approach
docs.text:
• Learn all 12 keys in Position A, then Position B
  • Practice in ii-V-I patterns around the Circle of Fifths
  • See Chord Voicing Principles for universal voicing principles that apply here too

docs.title: Pillar — Chords
docs.text: Chords are the harmonic backbone of jazz. Knowing how they're built and how they move gives you the foundation for comping, improvisation, and arrangement.

docs.title: Triads
docs.text:
Name Intervals Example Semitones Major M3 + P5 C E G 4, 3 Minor m3 + P5 C Eb G 3, 4 Diminished m3 + d5 C Eb Gb 3, 3 Augmented M3 + A5 C E G# 4, 4

See Intervals for interval definitions.

docs.text:
The six chord types that matter in jazz:

Name Symbol Notes (C) Semitones Role Major 7 Cmaj7, CΔ C E G B 4,3,4 I of major ii-V-I Minor 7 Cm7 C Eb G Bb 3,4,3 ii of major ii-V-I Dominant 7 C7 C E G Bb 4,3,3 V of any ii-V-I Minor-major 7 CmΔ C Eb G B 3,4,4 I of minor ii-V-I Half-diminished Cø, Cm7b5 C Eb Gb Bb 3,3,4 ii of minor ii-V-I Diminished 7 C°7 C Eb Gb A 3,3,3 Passing/substitute

For full symbol notation (including extensions, alterations, slash chords): Chord Symbols.

docs.title: Extensions
docs.text:
Extensions are built from the chord's major scale — count up 9, 11, or 13 from the root. They can be added to any 7th chord:

  • 9th — adds color (Dm9, Cmaj9, G9)
  • 11th — adds tension; use #4 notation for maj7 chords, 11 for min7/V7
  • 13th — only V7 chords use "13" symbol (maj/min chords call it the "6th")
  • Altered extensions (b9, #9, #11, b13) — V7 chords only; see Altered Dominants

docs.title: Core progressions
docs.text:
ii-V-I (major): Dm7 — G7 — Cmaj7 ii-V-I (minor): Dø — G7(b9) — Cm I-IV-V (blues): C7 — F7 — G7 iii-VI-ii-V: Em7 — A7 — Dm7 — G7

The ii-V-I is the single most important progression in jazz. Almost every standard is built from chains of ii-V-I progressions.

docs.title: How chords are voiced
docs.text: Chord symbols tell you what notes — voicings tell you how to arrange them. See Pillar - Voicings for the full system, and Chord Voicing Principles for the underlying rules.

docs.title: Pillar
docs.text: The Improv Pillar is focused on developing your improvisational skills, which are crucial in jazz music. Jazz is known for its freedom of expression, and improvisation allows you to play spontaneously over the harmonic framework of a song. This pillar will teach you how to approach improvisation, build your skills, and use various techniques to create interesting and expressive solos.

docs.title: What is Improvisation?
docs.text: Improvisation in jazz is the art of spontaneously creating melodies and solos over the chord changes of a song. It involves understanding the harmony, rhythm, and melody of a piece, and using your creativity to add to it in real-time. As a beginner, you'll start by learning how to improvise using scales, arpeggios, and chord tones, and eventually build your ability to create more complex and interesting lines.

docs.title: Key Elements of Jazz Improvisation
docs.text:
• Harmony: Understanding the chord progressions and how to use the chords' tones in your improvisation.
  • Rhythm: Incorporating syncopation, swing rhythms, and phrasing to make your solos feel dynamic.
  • Melody: Creating memorable, singable lines that follow the structure of the song and connect with the listener.
  • Expression: Using dynamics, articulation, and phrasing to give your solo emotional depth and style.

docs.title: Approaching Improvisation
docs.text:
1. Start Simple: When you're first learning to improvise, start with simple ideas. Use a single scale or arpeggio over a chord, and create short, simple phrases. As you progress, you'll learn how to connect these phrases into longer, more complex solos.

  2. Chord Tones: Focus on using the chord tones (1st, 3rd, 5th, and 7th of the chord) as the foundation of your improvisation. These are the notes that define the harmony and will give your improvisation a strong, grounded feel.

  3. Using Scales: Learn how to use the appropriate scales for each chord in the progression. This will help you navigate through the changes and create smooth, connected lines. For example, if the chord is Cmaj7, you can use the C major scale to improvise, and for G7, you might use the G Mixolydian scale.

  4. Develop Motifs: A motif is a short musical idea or phrase that you can repeat and vary throughout your solo. This can help create unity and coherence in your improvisation, making your solo feel more structured and intentional.

  5. Phrasing: Just like speaking, phrasing is an essential part of improvisation. Break up your solo into phrases, with breathing spaces between them. Think of each phrase as a "sentence" that expresses a musical idea.

  6. Listening: Listening to great jazz musicians and learning from their solos is crucial for developing your own voice. Pay attention to how they phrase their lines, use dynamics, and create tension and release in their solos.

docs.title: 1. Playing on the Chord Changes
docs.text: • When improvising, focus on the chord tones and the changes happening in the song. You can approach a chord change by highlighting the chord tones or the root of the new chord to make the transition smoother.

docs.title: 2. Using Scales and Modes
docs.text:
• Each chord in a progression has a corresponding scale or mode. Start by learning the common modes used in jazz, like the major scale (Ionian), the minor scale (Aeolian), and the Mixolydian scale for dominant chords.
  • For example, when improvising over a Cmaj7 chord, you could use the C major scale (C Ionian), and over a G7 chord, you could use the G Mixolydian scale.

docs.title: 3. Embellishing with Passing Tones
docs.text: • Passing tones (or passing notes) are notes that connect chord tones. These notes can help you create smoother, more connected lines. Use passing tones strategically between chord tones to add movement to your improvisation.

docs.title: 4. Using Arpeggios
docs.text: • Arpeggios are the broken versions of the chords, and they can be a great tool for improvisation. Practice improvising using the arpeggios of the chords you’re playing over. For example, if you're playing over a Cmaj7 chord, you can use the Cmaj7 arpeggio (C, E, G, B) to create interesting solo lines.

docs.title: Why Practice Improvisation?
docs.text:
• Personal Expression: Improvisation is one of the most direct ways to express your musical ideas and emotions.
  • Jazz Language: Improvising helps you develop your "jazz language" — a set of musical phrases and ideas that you can draw upon when playing.
  • Flexibility: Learning to improvise makes you more adaptable as a musician, allowing you to play with other musicians and in different musical situations.
  • Creativity: It encourages creative thinking, as you'll always be creating new and original ideas on the spot.

docs.title: Pillar — Lead Sheets
docs.text: A lead sheet gives you the melody (standard notation) and chord symbols. Everything else — harmony, rhythm, arrangement — is your interpretation. Reading lead sheets fluently is the gateway to the jazz repertoire.

docs.title: The two skills
docs.text:
1. Reading chord symbols — knowing what every symbol means (see Chord Symbols for the full reference)

2. Voicing the chords — translating symbols into actual notes on the piano using your voicing toolkit (see Melody-Matching for the technique)

docs.title: Chord symbol quick reference
docs.text:
Symbol type Examples What it means Major 7 Cmaj7, CΔ, C^ C E G B Minor 7 Cm7, C-7 C Eb G Bb Dominant 7 C7 C E G Bb Minor-major 7 CmΔ C Eb G B Half-diminished Cø, Cm7b5 C Eb Gb Bb Diminished 7 C°7 C Eb Gb A 6th chord C6, Cm6 6th replaces 7th; Cm6 = A natural (not Ab) Suspended 4th C7sus4 4th replaces 3rd Extensions C9, Cm11, C13 Built from major scale Altered C7(b9), C7(#9), C7alt Altered extensions on V7 only Slash chord Bb/C Bb chord, C in bass

Full reference: Chord Symbols. For altered dominants specifically: Altered Dominants.

docs.title: How to play from a lead sheet
docs.text:
1. Identify the chord symbol — what chord type and extensions?
  2. Look at the melody note — what scale degree is it relative to the chord?
  3. Choose a voicing — use Melody-Matching to pick the voicing whose top note is the melody note
  4. Play chord and melody together — the melody sits on top of the voicing

As you move through a song, alternate voicing types naturally (shells, open, rootless, etc.) for variety.

docs.title: The Real Book
docs.text: Most jazz standards are published in the Real Book — a collection of lead sheets. The notation uses the chord symbols covered in Chord Symbols. Standards are written in 4/4, 3/4, or occasionally 5/4. The head (melody) is played once through, then soloists improvise over the chord changes, then the head is repeated.

docs.title: Scales Pillar
docs.text: The Scales Pillar forms the foundation of jazz piano practice, enhancing finger dexterity, improving your understanding of key relationships, and developing a strong sense of rhythm. This is essential for mastering jazz improvisation and harmony.

docs.title: Why Practice Scales?
docs.text:
• Develop finger strength and control.
  • Build muscle memory for key relationships.
  • Lay the groundwork for improvisation and chord voicings.

docs.title: Connecting Scales and Cadences
docs.text: As you progress through different scales, understanding how they interact with cadences is crucial for creating musical phrases that feel complete. Cadences are chord progressions that signal the end of a phrase, section, or piece of music. They help define the structure and resolution within your playing.

docs.title: Why Cadences Matter
docs.text: Cadences provide a sense of closure (or lack thereof) depending on their form. By practicing scales and understanding their relationship to cadences, you'll develop a deeper understanding of harmony and how chords resolve naturally within a key. While scales focus on individual notes and intervals, cadences focus on the relationships between chords and how they guide the music forward or conclude it.

docs.title: Types of Cadences
docs.text:
There are two main categories of cadences: - Finished Cadences: These provide a sense of full resolution, always ending on the tonic (I). - Unfinished Cadences: These leave the phrase feeling open-ended and never end on the tonic (I).

For more details on the various types of cadences and how to use them effectively in your music, check out the cadence document.

Mastering scales will not only improve your technical abilities but also give you the confidence to explore jazz standards and improvisation. Start with C Major & Minor#Scales and work your way through the keys.

docs.title: Pillar — Voicings
docs.text: Voicings are how you arrange the notes of a chord on the piano. The same chord can sound sparse or lush, open or tight, simple or complex — all depending on voicing. Mastering voicings is what separates generic-sounding piano from a professional jazz sound.

docs.title: The five voicing types (beginner → advanced)
docs.text:
# Voicing Notes Hands Character 1 Shell Voicings Root, 3rd, 7th One Bare, minimal 2 Open Voicings 1 5 3 7 (spread) Two Clean, spacious, with bass 3 Rootless Voicings 3 5 7 9 Two Colorful, sophisticated — most used 4 Kenny Barron Voicing 1 5 9 / 3 7 11 Two Modern, stacked 5ths, requires stretch 5 Fourth Voicings 7 3 6 / 9 5 1 Two Quartal, open, post-bop

Learn them in order. Spend a week on each before moving to the next. See Chord Voicing Principles for the three universal principles all voicings follow.

docs.title: Choosing which voicing to use
docs.text: Use Melody-Matching: look at the melody note, identify its scale degree, pick a voicing that has that note on top. This is how you play chord and melody together in one hand position.

docs.title: Voice leading
docs.text: A good voicing sequence minimizes finger movement between chords. The 3rd and 7th of each chord (guide tones) tend to resolve smoothly to the 7th and 3rd of the next chord, especially in ii-V-I progressions. Shell voicings make this most obvious; rootless voicings extend the same principle with more color.
